bullets.summary: NVDA reported Q1 FY2026 revenue of $44.1 billion (up 12% QoQ), beating estimates by ~2%, but took a $4.5B charge on H20 inventory and purchase obligations after new U.S. export licensing requirements curtailed China demand. Gross margin ex-charge would have been 71.3% vs. reported 61.0%.

Q2 FY2026 guidance set at $45.0B (plus or minus 2%), but explicitly flags an ~$8.0B headwind from lost H20 China revenue due to export controls; the company expects mid-70% gross margins by year-end and mid-30% opex growth for full FY2026.

Alphabet Q1 2025 revenue grew 12% year-over-year; Google Cloud posted $12.3B in revenue for Q1 2025, up 28% YoY. The earnings call occurred April 29, 2025 and confirmed AI-driven cloud adoption was accelerating, with management citing strong enterprise demand for Gemini-powered services.

CEO Sundar Pichai reaffirmed Alphabet's $75B capex plan for 2025, predominantly for AI compute and data-center infrastructure, signaling no let-up in AI investment even as some analysts began raising demand-sustainability questions.

Microsoft Q3 FY2025 (quarter ended March 31, 2025, reported April 30, 2025): revenue $70.1B (+13% YoY), Azure cloud up 33% YoY, Intelligent Cloud segment $26.8B (+21%). Net income impacted by a $583M OpenAI investment loss in the quarter.

Microsoft Q3 FY2025 beat was driven by Azure's 33% growth and $42.4B total cloud revenue; CapEx was $21.4B for the quarter. Analysts flagged that free-cash-flow growth is compressing as AI infrastructure build-out costs intensify.

Rocket Lab Q1 2025 (reported May 8, 2025): revenue $122.6M (+32% YoY), at the top of guidance; gross margin 28.8% vs. 26.1% a year ago. Space Systems revenue rose 44.8% YoY. Q2 2025 guidance: $130M-$140M.

Rocket Lab signed new Neutron launch contract with the U.S. Air Force for a point-to-point transportation experiment (launch no earlier than 2026), and was awarded a HASTE contract by Kratos for the DoD MACH-TB 2.0 hypersonic program, demonstrating expanding government backlog.

May 2025 robotics industry highlights (The Robot Report): DHL plans to deploy 1,000+ Boston Dynamics Stretch robots by 2030; Amazon deployed Vulcan robots with force-and-touch sensing in warehouses; Automate 2025 (Detroit) and Robotics Summit (Boston) generated significant industry momentum. Humanoid deployments continued to receive heavy investment.

Persona AI raised $27M in pre-seed funding to develop humanoid robots for shipbuilding/manufacturing; igus unveiled Iggy Rob, a low-cost humanoid for industrial use; the global robotics market was estimated at $108B in 2025 with projections to $416B by 2035.

ARK Invest published a 'Know Your Genomic Exposure' white paper in May 2025, signaling continued focus on gene editing, multiomics, and precision medicine. ARKG's top holdings include CRISPR Therapeutics (~10%), Twist Bioscience (~9.7%), and Tempus AI (~9.6%), with gene editing expanding into cardiovascular disease and cancer indications.

ARK added a Multiomics Research Analyst in May 2025, reflecting deepening research commitment to integrating genomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic signals. ARKG posted roughly 23% NAV return for full-year 2025 after sharp declines in prior years, though the fund remained well below its 2021 peak.

Defiance QTUM ETF tracks the BlueStar Quantum Computing and Machine Learning Index (83-85 holdings, modified equal-weight). The fund had returned 50.54% in 2024, entering 2025 with strong momentum from Google Willow (105-qubit chip, Dec 2024) and IBM's Nighthawk processor milestones. By October 2025 it surpassed $3B AUM.

Quantum Computing Report's 2025 analysis noted that 2024 was 'the year of quantum roadmaps' with 13 players announcing or updating development paths; IBM continued progress toward useful quantum systems, with the 300mm wafer fabrication shift expanding production scale. No commercial revenue milestones for quantum hardware were recorded in the May 2025 window.

In May 2025, President Trump signed four executive orders (including EO 14300 on May 23, 2025) streamlining NRC review timelines: 18-month cap for new reactor construct-and-operate applications, 12-month cap for license renewals. A Standard Design Approval was issued for the NuScale US 460 (462 MW SMR) two months ahead of schedule.

AI data-center electricity demand narrative is the primary catalyst for NUKZ: tech hyperscalers are directing capital toward nuclear offtake agreements to meet 24/7 clean power requirements. NUKZ's ecosystem-wide exposure (reactors, construction, utilities, fuel) positions it to capture this theme broadly.

Gold reached an all-time nominal high of approximately $3,500/oz in late April 2025 and held near record levels through May 2025. The Fed held rates at 4.25%-4.50% at its May FOMC meeting, adding language about rising risks of both higher unemployment and higher inflation ('stagflation' framing), which supported gold as a hedge.

Gold year-to-date gain through May 2025 was approximately 27%. Central bank demand (China, others) and geopolitical uncertainty were cited as structural tailwinds distinct from prior rate cycles, helping IAU maintain historically elevated net asset values.

AGG (iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF) tracks investment-grade U.S. bonds. With the Fed on hold at 4.25%-4.50% through May 2025 and the 30-day SEC yield near 4.2%, AGG was generating modest income but facing duration headwinds. Elevated rate uncertainty driven by tariff-inflation risk weighed on intermediate-duration bond prices.

BIL (SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF) continued to benefit from the Fed's hold at 4.25%-4.50% in May 2025, delivering annualized yields near the policy rate with near-zero duration risk. Served as an effective cash proxy with competitive real yield in an uncertain rate environment.

VIG (Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF) tracks S&P U.S. Dividend Growers Index (10+ consecutive years of dividend growth). The fund's quality-tilt and low expense ratio make it a core equity holding; no material fund-level events in May 2025 beyond broader equity market recovery from the April tariff-shock drawdown.

AI.rationale: All three AI tickers showed strong YoY revenue growth with real enterprise adoption (Azure +33%, Google Cloud +28%, NVDA $44B revenue), and capex plans are being executed at scale. The H20 export restriction is a policy headwind for NVDA, but demand is described as supply-constrained, not demand-constrained. Analyst concern about free-cash-flow compression and overbuild is present but has not yet become the dominant narrative — adoption is still compounding with real, measurable revenue. This is surge, not yet peak.

rockets_spacecraft.rationale: RKLB showed 32% YoY revenue growth with margin improvement (gross margin 28.8%), beat the top of its guidance, and is winning new government contracts (Air Force, DoD MACH-TB). The backlog is growing. Revenue is real and accelerating, consistent with early-to-mid surge phase.

robotics.rationale: May 2025 robotics news was dominated by pre-seed and seed-stage funding (Persona AI $27M), product prototype launches, and industry conference activity. Major deployments (DHL/Boston Dynamics, Amazon Vulcan) exist but are still pilots or multi-year roll-out plans, not large-scale commercialization. Revenue from humanoids specifically is not yet material. This is buildup to early surge.

engineered_biology.rationale: ARKG's ETF reflects a thesis-driven position in companies still pre-commercialization or early-revenue. ARK's publishing of a 'Know Your Genomic Exposure' white paper and adding research staff signals deepening conviction but not broad market adoption. The fund posted ~23% for full 2025 but remained far from prior highs, suggesting a recovery off a digestion trough rather than a confirmed surge. Treating as late buildup.

quantum.rationale: No commercial quantum hardware revenue milestones appeared in the May 2025 window. Research roadmaps are advancing (IBM Nighthawk, Google Willow from late 2024), but the Quantum Computing Report explicitly noted 2024 was 'the year of roadmaps' — implying commercial reality is still ahead. QTUM's strong 2024 return (+50%) reflects investor enthusiasm more than underlying revenues, characteristic of early buildup/speculative buildup.

nuclear_fusion.rationale: Note: NUKZ tracks the broader nuclear energy renaissance (fission-heavy: SMRs, utilities, construction) rather than fusion specifically, but maps to the nuclear_fusion bucket per the profile's wave vocabulary. The May 2025 Executive Orders streamlining NRC review and the NuScale SMR design approval signal genuine regulatory tailwinds. AI-datacenter demand is a credible catalyst. But no commercial SMR has yet been commissioned in the U.S. This is classic policy-driven buildup.

general_markets.rationale: AGG, BIL, IAU, and VIG are macro/asset-class instruments with no single-wave attachment. Gold's record high and the Fed's hold at 4.25-4.50% are notable macro context, but these instruments do not map to a technology wave cycle.

thematic_concerns.concern: H20 export ban creates a structural $8B/quarter revenue gap and suppresses gross margins; policy risk is now a first-order risk factor, not a tail risk.
thematic_concerns.evidence: NVDA Q1 FY2026: $4.5B inventory charge and Q2 guidance explicitly flags ~$8B H20 revenue loss; CNBC reported the April 15, 2025 H20 licensing requirement.

thematic_concerns.concern: OpenAI investment losses reduced net income by $583M in Q3 FY2025; as OpenAI's burn rate is high and the investment is marked-to-model, this is an opaque recurring drag.
thematic_concerns.evidence: Microsoft Q3 FY2025 press release noted OpenAI losses reduced diluted EPS by $0.08.
